subject: [PATCH v5 14/36] mm/damon: skip private node memory in DAMON migration and pageout

2026-07-20.patch_summary: This patch modifies the DAMON (Data Access Monitor) kernel module to skip private node memory during migration and pageout operations. Private node memory is not reclaimable by default, so this change ensures that DAMON does not attempt to migrate or reclaim such memory. The patch adds checks for private node memory in both the migration and pageout paths of DAMON's code.

reviews.author: SJ Park
reviews.summary:
Reviewer SJ Park questioned whether private-node folios are truly reclaimable by default and requested a check to confirm this; they also asked about the potential consequences of attempting to page out private node memory.

Reviewer noted that the patch does not handle private node memory correctly, as it is not reclaimable by default and migration to such nodes is also unsupported.
reviews.raw_body:
"by default".  Does that mean it could be reclaimable in some situations?  If
so, could we check if it is reclaimable?

Also, what happens if we just try paging out the private node memory?  Will it
simply fail?  Or, make some problems?

---

Thanks,
SJ

2026-07-21.patch_summary: This patch modifies the DAMON (Data Access Monitor) kernel module to skip private node memory during migration and pageout operations. Private node memory is not reclaimable by default, so this change ensures that DAMON does not attempt to migrate or reclaim such memory. The modification involves adding checks for private node memory in both the migration and pageout paths of the DAMON code.

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that private node memory is not reclaimable by default and explained why it's skipped in DAMON migration and pageout paths, no fix planned

Author acknowledged that private node folios are handled similarly to ZONE_DEVICE folios, but expressed uncertainty about DAMON's handling of zone device pages.

Author acknowledged that migration operations to private nodes are allowed, but explained that operations on private node folios are refused unless a capability bit is set.
reviews.raw_body:
See the damon changes in:

https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/al-pkvmgIxGu3LzM@gourry-fedora-PF4VCD3F/T/#mfb99303c85dd9d4c3f58832dc28fc55583a7217a

---

user controls for MADV_ commands that affect private node folios would
skip those folios - exactly same as ZONE_DEVICE folios.

Which I'm not sure damon actually handles correctly presently (zone
device).  I remember looking around damon and thinking to myself "what
if this is a zone device page?" and not being able to find a satisfying
answer.

---

Technically there is nothing in migration core to prevent migration
operations, it's done on a service basis - hotunplug, reclaim/demotion,
user numa (mbind, migrate/move_pages) etc.

Operations on private nodes/private node folioes are refused if the
capability bit is not set.

~Gregory

reviews.author: SJ Park
reviews.summary:
Reviewer SJ Park noted that the 'by default' phrase in the code may cause confusion, as later patches will make private node memory optionally reclaimable and update this restriction by patch 26.

Reviewer SJ Park expressed uncertainty about the necessity of skipping private node memory in DAMON migration and pageout, asking if this blocking is conditional or will be allowed by a later patch.
reviews.raw_body:
Summarizing what you want to say, quoting something from the patch, or at least
calling it "26th patch of this series" would have made reviewing much easier.
Cc-ing damon@ for only patches that toucing DAMON source files and the cover
letter of the series could also be helpful.  Please consider doing some of
these for future replies.

So, I understand later patches will make it optionally reclaimable and update
this restriction by the 26th patch?  That sounds fair.  But, could we drop "by
default" from the above comment for reducing the confusion?

---

I haven't had a chance to read the entire series, sorry about that.  So, do you
mean the above blocking is not really needed, or that will conditionally be
allowed by another later patch?


Thanks,
SJ

[...]

reviews.author: Gregory Price (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that trimming patch descriptions is necessary for clarity and agreed to do so in the future.

Author acknowledged that the two-step process in the original patch is poor form and agreed to restructure it by introducing new node states, which will simplify the code and eliminate the need for an intermediate step.

Author acknowledges the need for further revision and promises to restructure the patch in v6, addressing multiple concerns raised by reviewer SJ Park.
reviews.raw_body:
I have been very much discouraged from this kind of trimming because it
removes the context of the series from the individual patches - making it
even more confusing.

But I understand, I will try to remember to do this for you.

---

Yes, but you bring up a good point - I think this two-step process is
actually poor form and that I need to redo it.

Maybe this can be redone as node states (features?) such as:

   N_MEMORY_RECLAIMABLE
   N_MEMORY_DEMOTABLE
   N_MEMORY_TIERABLE

so these damon changes will forego this confusing intermediate step of:

   if (folio_is_private_node(folio))

and go straight to

   if (node_is_reclaimable(folio_nid(folio)))

In the first patch - without ever having to revisit damon again.

---

I will reduce this all to a single patch in v6, I see where things can
be improved now based on a few pieces of feedback.

Thanks SJ!
~Gregory

2026-07-22.patch_summary: This patch modifies the DAMON (Data Access Monitor) kernel module to skip private node memory during migration and pageout operations. Private node memory is not reclaimable by default, so this change ensures that DAMON does not attempt to migrate or reclaim such memory. The modification involves adding checks for private node memory in both the migration and pageout paths of the DAMON code.

reviews.author: SJ Park
reviews.summary:
Reviewer suggested that instead of selective Cc-ing, a tool could be used to simplify the process, and proposed three alternative methods for reviewing: summarizing thoughts, quoting relevant text, or using a descriptive title.

Reviewer noted that private node memory is not reclaimable by default and migration is also unsupported, suggesting these checks should be added
reviews.raw_body:
Thank you, Gregory.  Nonetheless, I wouldn't insist selective Cc-ing.  I agree
your point, and I expect doing that would be tedious if you don't have a script
or tool (I use a tool).  It is just one of my suggestion to help reviewing in
this context, insted of only lore link.  Just one among the other three
suggestions (summarizing your thought, quoting, or simply calling it "26th
patch of this series") should also suffice.

---

Sounds good, thank you Gregory.  Looking forward to the next version!


Thanks,
SJ

[...]
